name: APT29 Cozy Bear Spear Phishing Campaign
description: A targeted spear phishing campaign modeled after APT29 (Cozy Bear) tactics used in the SolarWinds compromise. The attack begins with a spear phishing email containing a malicious link disguised as a security update, leading to credential harvesting via an EnvyScout HTML smuggling dropper, followed by Cobalt Strike beacon deployment, internal reconnaissance, and data exfiltration via encrypted C2 channels.

phases.name: Spear Phishing Email Delivery (EnvyScout)
phases.description: APT29 sends a spear phishing email impersonating a trusted organization (e.g., USAID, Microsoft) with an HTML attachment using the EnvyScout HTML smuggling technique to bypass email security gateways. The email targets the HR department with a fake benefits portal update.

expected_logs.description: Incoming email from external sender with HTML attachment
User opens HTML attachment triggering JavaScript blob download
DNS resolution for typosquatted domain micros0ft-update.com
mshta.exe or wscript.exe spawned from browser process
HTTPS connection to C2 infrastructure on port 443

indicators.subject: Action Required: Critical Security Update for Employee Portal

phases.name: Credential Harvesting via Fake OAuth Portal
phases.description: The phishing link redirects to a convincing replica of the Microsoft 365 OAuth consent page. The victim enters corporate credentials, which are captured and sent to APT29's collection infrastructure. The page also requests OAuth token permissions to maintain persistent access.

expected_logs.description: POST request with credentials to phishing domain
TLS connection to 185.220.101.42 on port 443
OAuth consent grant logged in Azure AD
Suspicious OAuth application consent from unusual IP

phases.name: Cobalt Strike Beacon Deployment
phases.description: Using the harvested credentials, APT29 deploys a Cobalt Strike beacon via a malicious ISO file delivered through the compromised email account. The ISO contains a hidden DLL (NativeZone loader) that is side-loaded when the user opens a decoy document inside the mounted ISO image.
phases.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Windows Command Shell

expected_logs.description: rundll32.exe loading NativeZone DLL from mounted ISO
ISO file mounted creating virtual drive
DLL side-loading via legitimate signed binary
HTTPS beacon to Cobalt Strike C2 on 45.77.182.18
Registry persistence key added under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run

phases.name: Active Directory Reconnaissance
phases.description: APT29 uses built-in Windows tools and the ADFind utility to enumerate the Active Directory environment, identify domain administrators, trust relationships, and high-value targets. They also run BloodHound to map attack paths to Domain Admin.

expected_logs.description: AdFind.exe executed for domain enumeration
nltest.exe /dclist and /domain_trusts commands
LDAP queries to domain controller on port 389
SharpHound.exe collector executed for BloodHound
BloodHound ZIP output written to temp directory

phases.name: Sensitive Data Collection
phases.description: APT29 accesses sensitive HR and financial documents from network shares and SharePoint, targeting employee PII, salary data, and strategic documents. Data is staged in a hidden directory before exfiltration.

expected_logs.description: Access to sensitive HR directories via SMB
Bulk file read operations on \\srv-db-01\hr-confidential
7zip.exe archiving staged files with password
Archive file created in C:\Users\Public\Downloads

phases.name: Data Exfiltration via Encrypted C2 Channel
phases.description: APT29 exfiltrates the staged data through the Cobalt Strike HTTPS beacon, splitting data into small chunks to avoid DLP detection. They also use legitimate cloud services (OneDrive API) as a secondary exfiltration channel, a technique characteristic of Nobelium operations.

expected_logs.description: Sustained HTTPS beacon traffic to 45.77.182.18 with increasing payload sizes
Chunked data transfer via Cobalt Strike named pipe
Microsoft Graph API calls to OneDrive for secondary exfil
DNS queries to graph.microsoft.com at unusual frequency
PowerShell invoking Invoke-WebRequest to upload chunks

expected_detection_points: HTML smuggling attachment with embedded JavaScript blob
Suspicious OAuth consent grant from external IP
ISO file mount followed by DLL side-loading via rundll32.exe
Cobalt Strike HTTPS beacon pattern (malleable C2 profile)
Bulk access to sensitive file shares outside business hours
Chunked HTTPS exfiltration to known threat infrastructure
Microsoft Graph API abuse for data exfiltration
